sections.title: Current Research
paragraphs: I am a scientific employee in the Department of Machine Dynamics and Acoustics at TH Wildau. My current work investigates acoustic and vibration-based condition monitoring of rail-vehicle components, with emphasis on loose bolted joints and structural defects. Related outputs include the Forum Acusticum preprint and the DAGA SIAMIS paper.

In parallel, I conduct doctoral research at the Chair of Acoustics and Haptics at TU Dresden. My PhD work focuses on acoustic vehicle alerting systems for electric trucks. Key outputs include the JAES journal article, the electric-truck masking study, and conference papers on semantic AVAS modeling, warning-sound design, and electric-truck AVAS requirements. This work also includes the ICSV32 proceedings paper on annoyance in multi-source AVAS environments.

sections.title: Engineering Background
paragraphs: My background bridges research and product-oriented engineering. I have worked on turboshaft and turboprop engine design at GE Aerospace, flexible electronic sensors at Kordsa, hospital-monitoring and IoT systems as a start-up co-founder, and engineering software development in the automotive and aerospace sectors. The medical-device work produced three linked patent records: a nasogastric-tube system, an invasive device, and an infusion-pump detection system.

At the Bio-Integrated Microdevices Laboratory at Koç University, I worked on flexible piezoelectric energy harvesters and ultrasonic transducer modeling. The resulting outputs include an Integrated Ferroelectrics article, a wind-energy harvester article, my master's thesis, and an IEEE IUS paper.

sections.title: Research Approach
paragraphs: I work from measurement to interpretation: define the engineering question, design a controlled experiment, acquire reproducible data, apply statistical or signal-processing models, and translate the result into a design or diagnostic decision.

This approach has led to peer-reviewed work across acoustics, vibration perception, energy harvesting, and ultrasonic transducers. My Scientific Reports article on whole-body vibration perception is one example. The complete publication list is available on the Publications & CV page and on Google Scholar.

footer.aboutText: Acoustics researcher and mechanical engineer working on psychoacoustics, vehicle sound, vibro-acoustic diagnostics, sensing, and experimental engineering.

projects.title: Selected Research & Engineering Work
projects.subtitle: Projects spanning psychoacoustics, condition monitoring, sensing, and engineering development

items.title: Electric Truck AVAS Sound Design
items.subtitle: User-centered psychoacoustic development of warning sounds
items.description: Designed and evaluated acoustic vehicle alerting systems for electric trucks using semantic profiling, psychoacoustic listening tests, binaural synthesis, and sound-quality analysis.

items.title: Psychoacoustic Modeling of Vehicle Sounds
items.subtitle: Connecting signal metrics with semantic perception
items.description: Developed consumer-centered semantic attributes and perceptual models for electric-vehicle sounds using listening-test data, psychoacoustic metrics, statistics, and multivariate analysis.

items.title: Electric-Truck Masking Thresholds
items.subtitle: Audibility limits in realistic vehicle interiors
items.description: Experimentally determined auditory masking thresholds in electric-truck cabin noise to support the design of audible yet non-intrusive warning and information sounds.

items.title: Bolted-Joint Condition Monitoring
items.subtitle: Vibro-acoustic detection of looseness and nonlinear behavior
items.description: Investigated how tightening torque and loosened joints affect acoustic and vibration responses, including single-tone and frequency-modulated excitation for rail-component diagnostics.

items.title: Flexible Piezoelectric Energy Harvesters
items.subtitle: PVDF-TrFE devices for structural-health monitoring
items.description: Designed, modeled, fabricated, and tested flexible polymer energy harvesters, combining acoustic-equivalent circuits, finite-element simulation, and micro/nanofabrication.

items.title: Medical IoT & Patented Monitoring Systems
items.subtitle: Connected devices for safer hospital workflows
items.description: Co-founded a hospital-digitalization start-up and developed sensing and communication concepts for infusion pumps, invasive devices, ventilation monitoring, and nasogastric-tube systems.

items.title: TH Wildau - Scientific Employee
items.description: Research in acoustic and vibration-based condition monitoring of rail-vehicle components, focusing on loose bolted joints and structural defects.

items.title: TU Dresden - Doctoral Researcher
items.description: Doctoral research on electric-truck AVAS sound design, psychoacoustic testing, semantic evaluation, warning-sound audibility, and binaural sound synthesis.

items.title: GE Aerospace - Design Engineer
items.description: Design engineering work on CT7/T700 turboshaft and turboprop engine programs.

items.title: TEDAVEM Information Systems - Co-Founder
items.description: Co-founded a hospital-digitalization start-up developing IoT monitoring concepts and integrated communication electronics for medical equipment.

items.title: Kordsa - Flexible Electronics Researcher
items.description: Worked in thin films and flexible electronic sensors; developed LabVIEW software for experimental testing.

items.title: Koç University - MSc Mechanical Engineering
items.description: Research in flexible polymer energy harvesters, PMUTs, micro/nanofabrication, and acoustic equivalent-circuit modeling.

items.title: Renault Group - Automotive R&D Intern
items.description: Developed a CATIA automation tool exceeding 20,000 lines of code to support manipulation of 3D parts in R&D workflows.

items.title: Koç University - BSc Mechanical Engineering
items.description: Completed undergraduate mechanical engineering studies and contributed to wearable sensor-system research for venous dislodgement detection.

conferencePresentations.title: Conference Presentations
conferencePresentations.subtitle: Recorded talks and presentation details from my work in acoustics, psychoacoustics, vibration, and sensing.
conferencePresentations.intro: Each entry includes the bibliographic information, conference context, a concise abstract, the associated paper, and a presentation-video area. Videos are provided in English.
conferencePresentations.videoLanguageNote: Presentation videos are in English in both language versions of this website.

items.title: Perceived Annoyance in Multi-Source Electric Vehicle AVAS Environments
items.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, Serkan Atamer, Jonas Krautwurm, and M. Ercan Altinsoy
items.conference: 32nd International Congress on Sound and Vibration (ICSV32)
items.session: T14 SS03: Sound Quality and Product Design
items.abstract: This study extends AVAS evaluation from isolated vehicle sounds to realistic multi-vehicle traffic scenes. Binaurally rendered single- and two-vehicle pass-by scenes were assessed in a listening test using perceived-annoyance ratings. Simultaneous two-vehicle scenes produced a significant increase in short-term annoyance compared with the single-vehicle baseline, showing that AVAS optimisation should consider scene-level interactions in addition to individual-source detectability.

items.title: Frequency-Modulated and Single-Tone Excitation to Reveal Vibro-Acoustic Nonlinearities in Loosened Bolted Joints
items.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, R. Pianowski, and D. Hannebauer
items.abstract: Loss of preload changes the stiffness, damping, and nonlinear response of a bolted joint. A laboratory demonstrator was investigated at several preload levels using a triaxial accelerometer and a microphone under single-tone and frequency-modulated excitation. Harmonic content and additional spectral components were evaluated as vibro-acoustic indicators for distinguishing loosened from highly preloaded states.

video.message: Presentation video will be added after the conference

items.title: SIAMIS: Experimental Study on the Acoustic Behavior of Bolted Joints Under Varying Tightening Torques
items.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, R. Kamenzky, and D. Hannebauer
items.abstract: Controlled impact tests were used to investigate the airborne and structure-borne acoustic responses of bolted specimens at different tightening torques. Microphone and accelerometer measurements were evaluated through transfer functions, resonance shifts, damping-related quantities, and sound-pressure trends. The study identifies preload-sensitive features that can support acoustic and vibration-based condition monitoring of bolted joints.

items.title: Psychoacoustic Modelling of AVAS Sounds: Consumer-Centric Semantic Attribute Development for Electric Vehicles
items.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, M. Engel, Serkan Atamer, and M. Ercan Altinsoy
items.abstract: The work develops consumer-centred semantic descriptors for Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System sounds and connects them to measurable psychoacoustic properties. AVAS samples were evaluated using semantic differential scales, and statistical modelling was used to determine which perceptual attributes distinguish preferred and non-preferred sounds. The resulting vocabulary supports the translation of user expectations into engineering-oriented sound-design targets.

items.title: Experimental Determination of the Masking Threshold in Electric Trucks
items.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, Oliver Zeidler, Serkan Atamer, and M. Ercan Altinsoy
items.abstract: This study examines how the interior noise of electric trucks masks tonal auditory cues. Binaural recordings at different driving speeds were reproduced under controlled laboratory conditions, and frequency-dependent detection thresholds were measured in listening tests. The results provide a basis for designing in-cabin warning sounds that remain detectable without becoming unnecessarily intrusive.

items.title: Designing Effective Warning Sounds: Psychoacoustical Considerations
items.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, Jin Wook Lee, Serkan Atamer, and M. Ercan Altinsoy
items.abstract: Warning sounds must attract attention and communicate urgency while avoiding excessive annoyance. Two listening-test studies examined how psychoacoustic characteristics influence perceived urgency and annoyance. The presentation discusses how temporal and spectral design parameters can be combined to create warning signals that are effective, interpretable, and acceptable to listeners.

items.title: Acoustic Vehicle Alerting Systems (AVAS) for Electric Trucks: Initial Thoughts for the Requirements
items.authors: Berkay Kullukçu, Robert Rosenkranz, and M. Ercan Altinsoy
items.abstract: A semantic-differential listening study compared internal-combustion and electric cars and trucks, both with and without AVAS. The analysis identified perceptual differences such as truck-likeness, effortlessness, whistling, and howling, and related these attributes to objective psychoacoustic properties. The findings form an initial requirements framework for designing electric-truck AVAS that communicate vehicle size and character without excessive annoyance.

items.title: Modeling Miniaturized Piezoelectric Ultrasound Transducers: Comparison of Lumped and Finite Element Models
items.authors: P. T. Ziarati, Berkay Kullukçu, and Levent Beker
items.abstract: This work compares lumped-element and finite-element modelling approaches for miniaturized piezoelectric ultrasound transducers intended for compact ultrasonic systems. The study evaluates the ability of the two approaches to predict electrical and acoustic behaviour while considering model complexity and computation effort, providing guidance for efficient transducer design and optimisation.
